questions.question: Explain the concept of runtime polymorphism in C++ and how virtual functions enable it.
keyPoints: Runtime polymorphism allows selecting at runtime which function to execute based on object type
Virtual functions use vtable and vptr mechanism
When a virtual function is called through base pointer/reference, the correct derived version is invoked at runtime
This is also called late binding or dynamic binding

questions.question: What is a pure virtual function? How does it differ from a regular virtual function? Give an example.
keyPoints: Pure virtual function has no implementation: virtual void func() = 0
It must be overridden by derived classes for instantiation
Class with pure virtual function becomes abstract
Regular virtual function has implementation that can be optionally overridden
Derived classes must implement all pure virtual functions or become abstract themselves

questions.question: Why is it necessary to use virtual destructors in polymorphic base classes? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: When base pointer deletes derived object, only base destructor executes if not virtual
This causes resource leaks and undefined behavior
Virtual destructor ensures derived destructor runs first, then base destructor
All destructors in inheritance chain are called in reverse order of construction
Essential for proper cleanup of derived class resources

questions.question: Explain the vtable and vptr mechanism used for implementing virtual functions.
keyPoints: vtable is a lookup table created per class with virtual functions
Contains pointers to virtual function implementations for that class
vptr is a hidden pointer in each object pointing to class vtable
When virtual function is called, vptr accesses vtable to get correct function address
This enables runtime determination of which function to execute

questions.question: What is the 'override' keyword in C++11? Why is it recommended to use it when overriding virtual functions?
keyPoints: override keyword explicitly marks a function as overriding a base class virtual function
Compiler checks if base class has matching virtual function to override
If no match, compilation error occurs - catches mistakes
Helps distinguish overriding from new function declaration
Makes code more readable and maintainable

questions.question: Define abstract class. Can abstract classes have constructors? Explain with reasoning.
keyPoints: Abstract class contains at least one pure virtual function
Cannot be instantiated directly - only used as base class
Can have constructors - used to initialize base class members
Derived class constructors call abstract class constructor
All pure virtual functions must be implemented to instantiate derived class

questions.question: Explain the problem of object slicing and how it can be avoided.
keyPoints: Slicing occurs when derived object is assigned to base object by value
All derived class-specific data and behaviors are lost
Only base class portion is copied
Avoided by using pointers or references to base class
Virtual functions work properly only with pointers/references, not sliced objects

questions.question: Write a C++ program demonstrating runtime polymorphism using virtual functions. Create a base class 'Shape' with virtual function 'area()' and derive classes 'Circle' and 'Rectangle'. Show how base pointer can call appropriate derived class functions.
keyPoints: Define Shape class with virtual area() function
Create Circle class with radius and overridden area() returning π*r*r
Create Rectangle class with length, breadth and overridden area() returning l*b
Demonstrate base pointer pointing to derived objects
Show correct area() is called based on actual object type at runtime
Include proper class definitions, constructors, and display output

questions.question: Explain the differences between early binding and late binding. How does virtual function achieve late binding?
keyPoints: Early binding: function call resolved at compile-time based on pointer/reference type
Late binding: function call resolved at runtime based on actual object type
Virtual functions use vtable and vptr for late binding
Non-virtual functions use early binding for performance
Trade-off: late binding has small runtime overhead but enables polymorphism
vptr added to objects, vtable created per class with virtual functions

questions.question: What is the diamond problem in multiple inheritance? How does virtual inheritance solve it? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: Diamond problem: ambiguity when two paths lead to same base class
Class D inherits from B and C, both inherit from A
Two copies of A in D causing ambiguity and redundancy
Virtual inheritance: use virtual keyword when inheriting B and C from A
Single instance of A shared by B and C through virtual inheritance
Constructors called in order: A first, then B and C, then D

questions.question: Design an abstract class 'Animal' with pure virtual function 'speak()'. Create derived classes 'Dog' and 'Cat' that implement the 'speak()' function. Write a complete C++ program demonstrating polymorphic behavior.
keyPoints: Animal class with pure virtual function: virtual void speak() = 0
Dog class overriding speak() to print "Woof!"
Cat class overriding speak() to print "Meow!"
Create Animal pointer array pointing to Dog and Cat objects
Demonstrate calling speak() through base pointer shows polymorphic behavior
Proper constructors and destructor handling
